stem:
A 38-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium carbonate 750 mg at night for 5 years with a steady-state trough level of 0.7 mmol/L. She attends her GP for a routine review. She is well, taking no other medications, with no recent illness. She drinks 2 standard drinks per week and does not smoke. Her last full blood count, electrolytes, urea, creatinine, eGFR, calcium, and TSH were taken 9 months ago and were within normal limits. She wants to know what monitoring she needs from now on while she remains on lithium.

She is not pregnant and is using long-acting reversible contraception.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 72/min, blood pressure 116/74 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Well, fine resting tremor of hands, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Lithium level 0.7 mmol/L 9 months ago; eGFR 84 mL/min/1.73m2; TSH 2.1 mU/L; calcium 2.42 mmol/L; FBC normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate ongoing monitoring schedule?

options.text: Annual lithium level with electrolytes and renal function only if symptoms of toxicity occur.
options.rationale: Routine surveillance must continue regardless of symptoms because lithium nephrotoxicity and thyroid dysfunction are insidious. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: psychotropic)

options.text: Lithium level every 3 months without further renal or endocrine monitoring needed during stability.
options.rationale: Monitoring lithium without checking eGFR, TSH, and calcium misses the very organ effects routine review is designed to detect. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: psychotropic)

options.text: Lithium level, electrolytes, and TSH every 6 months without parathyroid hormone or calcium tested.
options.rationale: Hypercalcaemia from lithium-induced hyperparathyroidism is a recognised complication that is missed without routine calcium. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: psychotropic)

options.text: Lithium level every 3 months with annual electrolytes, calcium, and TSH long term while on therapy.
options.rationale: Six-monthly is the recommended frequency for the panel when stable; staggering creates gaps in detection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: psychotropic)

options.text: Lithium level, electrolytes, eGFR, calcium, and TSH every 6 months long term.
options.rationale: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines specify 6-monthly lithium, U+E, eGFR, calcium, and TSH once stable, lifelong. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium therapy requires routine surveillance for the three classical organ effects: chronic kidney disease, hypothyroidism, and primary hyperparathyroidism with hypercalcaemia. Australian Therapeutic Guidelines: psychotropic recommend a steady-state trough lithium level plus electrolytes, eGFR, corrected calcium, and TSH every 6 months once a patient has been stable for at least 6 months, continued lifelong. Earlier monitoring (every 1 to 3 months) is reserved for initiation, dose change, or intercurrent illness with dehydration or new interacting medication.
key_points: Lifelong 6-monthly lithium, U+E, eGFR, corrected calcium, and TSH once stable.
Lithium can cause nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and chronic interstitial nephritis.
Hypercalcaemia from primary hyperparathyroidism is increasingly recognised on long-term lithium.
explanation.pearls: Any new interacting medication (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazides) or intercurrent illness with vomiting warrants a check at 5 to 7 days because lithium toxicity can develop in days.

stem:
A 24-year-old man with first-episode schizophrenia was commenced on olanzapine 10 mg at night 12 weeks ago by the community mental health team. He has gained 4.5 kg, has settled positive symptoms, and is functionally improving. His baseline metabolic screen at initiation was: BMI 25 kg/m2, fasting glucose 5.0 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.4 percent, total cholesterol 4.8 mmol/L, triglycerides 1.2 mmol/L, prolactin 320 mIU/L, ECG QTc 410 ms. He has no personal or family history of diabetes or cardiovascular disease and does not smoke. He drinks alcohol minimally.

He attends his community mental health appointment today and asks whether any tests are due.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 76/min, blood pressure 122/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7°C, weight 79 kg (up from 74.5 kg)
data_table.Examination: Well, no abnormal movements; abdominal girth increased; mood euthymic
data_table.Investigations: Baseline 12 weeks ago as listed in stem; no repeat bloods since starting olanzapine

lead_in: What is the most appropriate metabolic monitoring at this visit?

options.text: Repeat fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipid panel, and weight; recheck waist circumference and blood pressure.
options.rationale: RANZCP recommends fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipids, weight, waist, and blood pressure at 12 weeks and then at least annually on second-generation antipsychotics. (Source: RANZCP schizophrenia guideline)

options.text: Repeat only fasting glucose and HbA1c at this 12 week visit because lipids change more slowly.
options.rationale: Lipid abnormalities can emerge within 12 weeks of olanzapine and are part of routine monitoring at this visit. (Source: RANZCP schizophrenia guideline)

options.text: Defer metabolic monitoring until the 6 month review, focusing on symptom response in the meantime.
options.rationale: Twelve week metabolic monitoring is part of the AU schedule precisely because olanzapine commonly causes early metabolic change. (Source: RANZCP schizophrenia guideline)

options.text: Repeat lipid panel only; defer fasting glucose and HbA1c until the 24 week visit later this year.
options.rationale: Insulin resistance can develop early with olanzapine and screening at 12 weeks is recommended. (Source: RANZCP schizophrenia guideline)

options.text: Repeat ECG, prolactin, and metabolic panel together with assessment for tardive dyskinesia today.
options.rationale: Routine 12 week ECG and prolactin are not standard unless symptomatic; tardive dyskinesia surveillance starts later in treatment. (Source: RANZCP schizophrenia guideline)

explanation.summary: Second-generation antipsychotics, especially olanzapine and clozapine, drive weight gain, dyslipidaemia, and impaired glucose tolerance, often within the first 3 months. The RANZCP and Australian Therapeutic Guidelines schedule recommends measuring weight and waist circumference, blood pressure, fasting glucose or HbA1c, and a lipid panel at baseline, 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months, and annually thereafter. Olanzapine and clozapine carry the highest metabolic risk; lurasidone and aripiprazole carry the lowest.
key_points: 12 week monitoring includes weight, waist, BP, fasting glucose or HbA1c, and lipid panel.
Olanzapine and clozapine are the highest-risk antipsychotics for metabolic syndrome.
Switch to a lower-risk agent if HbA1c crosses 6.0 percent or weight gain exceeds 7 percent of baseline.
explanation.pearls: If HbA1c rises by more than 0.5 percent at 12 weeks, prompt a switch consideration rather than waiting for diabetes to develop on top of stable psychotic illness.

stem:
A 42-year-old man with a 25 pack-year history attends his GP wanting to stop smoking 25 cigarettes a day. He has tried nicotine patches twice in the past without success, lapsing back to smoking within 2 weeks each time. He has a quit date in 2 weeks. He has well-controlled hypertension on perindopril 5 mg daily, no significant mental health history, no current depressive symptoms (PHQ-9 score 3), and no history of seizures or eating disorder. He drinks 6 standard drinks per week. He has read about available options online and wants the most effective pharmacotherapy.

He is willing to combine pharmacotherapy with behavioural support through Quitline.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 74/min, blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Well, no respiratory distress, mild peripheral cyanosis of fingers from staining; chest clear
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 5.4 mmol/L; HbA1c 5.6 percent; spirometry FEV1/FVC 0.78 predicted

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Bupropion sustained release 150 mg daily for 3 days then twice daily, started 1 week before quit date.
options.rationale: Bupropion is second-line when varenicline is contraindicated, declined, or unavailable; varenicline has superior efficacy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: smoking cessation)

options.text: Varenicline starting at 0.5 mg daily titrated to 1 mg twice daily, started 1 week before quit date.
options.rationale: Varenicline has the highest efficacy as smoking cessation monotherapy in Australia and is first-line for committed quitters without contraindications. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: smoking cessation)

options.text: Combination nicotine replacement therapy with a 21 mg patch plus 4 mg lozenges for breakthrough cravings.
options.rationale: Combination NRT is reasonable but varenicline remains the most effective monotherapy in head-to-head trials. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: smoking cessation)

options.text: Nortriptyline 25 mg at night titrated up to 75 mg over a fortnight to suppress cigarette cravings.
options.rationale: Nortriptyline is a fourth-line option used only if other pharmacotherapies fail or are contraindicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: smoking cessation)

options.text: Nicotine inhaler used hourly during waking hours alongside Quitline behavioural counselling sessions.
options.rationale: Monotherapy with a single short-acting NRT is less effective than combination NRT or varenicline. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: smoking cessation)

explanation.summary: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines rank smoking cessation pharmacotherapy by efficacy: varenicline (now considered first-line again following resolution of historical safety concerns) is most effective, then combination nicotine replacement therapy (patch plus short-acting), then bupropion or single NRT, with nortriptyline as a fourth-line option. Varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks, started 1 week before quit date, is the standard schedule. Combine pharmacotherapy with structured behavioural support through Quitline (13 78 48).
key_points: Second-line: combination NRT (long-acting patch plus short-acting gum or lozenge).
Combine pharmacotherapy with structured behavioural support such as Quitline.
explanation.pearls: If the patient has active major depression or recent suicidal ideation, screen carefully before starting varenicline; current evidence does not show a population-level risk but individual histories warrant caution.

stem:
A 29-year-old female accountant presents to your psychiatry clinic for a second opinion. She describes lifelong difficulty sustaining attention on tasks she finds tedious, frequent careless errors at work, chronic disorganisation, time blindness, and emotional dysregulation. School reports from age 8 documented inattention, daydreaming, and incomplete homework. She has no childhood hyperactivity. Self-report scales show ASRS v1.1 part A six positive items out of six, and the DIVA-5 confirms five inattention symptoms with childhood onset, persistence, impairment in two domains, and exclusion of other better explanations. Her PHQ-9 is 4 and GAD-7 is 6. She has no psychotic symptoms, no current substance use, and her ECG is normal. She drives 30 minutes daily for work.

She has read about treatment options and is asking what comes next.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 72/min, blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Well groomed, normal affect, no abnormal movements, normal cardiovascular and neurological exam
data_table.Investigations: ECG QTc 410 ms; TFTs normal; FBC and U+E normal; urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Trial atomoxetine 40 mg daily for 6 weeks before considering any stimulant therapy as the first-line option.
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is reasonable when stimulants are contraindicated or declined but is not the routine first-line in adult ADHD. (Source: RANZCP adult ADHD guideline)

options.text: Refer for structured cognitive behavioural therapy alone over 6 months before any consideration of medication.
options.rationale: CBT augments treatment of adult ADHD but is not curative as monotherapy when stimulants are appropriate. (Source: RANZCP adult ADHD guideline)

options.text: Commence lisdexamfetamine 30 mg orally each morning, review in 4 weeks, and advise no driving during titration.
options.rationale: DSM-5 adult ADHD is established; first-line is a long-acting stimulant with driving restriction during titration per AU prescribing advice. (Source: RANZCP adult ADHD guideline)

options.text: Trial methylphenidate immediate release 5 mg three times daily without driving restrictions because doses are small.
options.rationale: Immediate release methylphenidate requires multiple daily doses and AU prescribing still advises driving caution during titration. (Source: RANZCP adult ADHD guideline)

options.text: Defer treatment and reassess in 6 months because her PHQ-9 and GAD-7 scores are too high for stimulant prescribing.
options.rationale: PHQ-9 of 4 and GAD-7 of 6 are not above thresholds that would defer ADHD treatment in this presentation. (Source: RANZCP adult ADHD guideline)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis under DSM-5 requires at least five inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms (out of nine each), childhood onset before age 12, persistence, impairment in at least two settings, and no better explanation. First-line treatment in Australia is a long-acting stimulant such as lisdexamfetamine 30 mg daily or methylphenidate ER, titrated weekly. Pre-treatment workup includes cardiovascular assessment with resting ECG. Patients must be advised that driving may be impaired during titration or dose changes, and to defer driving until they know their response; ongoing disclosure to the licensing authority is required only if a clinician judges fitness to drive is affected.
key_points: Long-acting stimulant is first-line for adult ADHD in Australia.
Baseline ECG and cardiovascular history are required before initiation.
Patients should not drive during titration or for 2 weeks after each dose change until response is known.
explanation.pearls: Document that the patient understands driving restrictions during titration and the obligation to self-report to the licensing authority if symptoms compromise safe driving; this is a recurrent medicolegal pitfall.

sources.label: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

stem:
A 32-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 90 minutes after ingesting an estimated 50 tablets of amitriptyline 25 mg (1250 mg total) with alcohol. She is drowsy but rousable. She has a known history of depression and previous overdose. On examination she is warm and dry with dilated pupils and absent bowel sounds. An ECG taken on arrival 10 minutes ago is on the trolley. She has been intubated for airway protection because of decreasing GCS and her arterial blood gas has returned.

The ED registrar is preparing the next intervention.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 132/min, blood pressure 90/55 mmHg, respiratory rate ventilated at 16/min, SpO2 99% on FiO2 0.4, temperature 37.4°C
data_table.Examination: Sedated, intubated; pupils dilated; warm and dry skin; absent bowel sounds; no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia, QRS 145 ms, terminal R in aVR 4 mm; ABG pH 7.28, PaCO2 38 mmHg, HCO3 18 mmol/L, lactate 3.2 mmol/L; potassium 3.6 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Amiodarone 300 mg intravenous bolus over 10 minutes to terminate the tachyarrhythmia.
options.rationale: Class IA, IC, and III antiarrhythmics are contraindicated in TCA poisoning because they prolong QT and worsen sodium channel blockade. (Source: AU TCA poisoning guideline)

options.text: Flumazenil 0.2 mg intravenously to reverse benzodiazepine-associated sedation contribution.
options.rationale: Flumazenil in mixed overdose risks precipitating seizures, particularly with TCA co-ingestion. (Source: AU TCA poisoning guideline)

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g via the nasogastric tube without prior bicarbonate therapy considered first.
options.rationale: Charcoal is only useful within 1 to 2 hours of ingestion and does not address sodium channel blockade cardiotoxicity. (Source: AU TCA poisoning guideline)

options.text: Sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg intravenously over 5 minutes targeting QRS narrowing and pH 7.45 to 7.55.
options.rationale: QRS above 100 ms or terminal R in aVR above 3 mm in TCA poisoning is an indication for sodium bicarbonate. (Source: AU TCA poisoning guideline)

options.text: Intravenous lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg as first-line therapy ahead of any other intervention.
options.rationale: Lipid emulsion is a rescue therapy for refractory cardiovascular collapse, not first-line in TCA poisoning. (Source: AU TCA poisoning guideline)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant cardiotoxicity is from fast sodium channel blockade in myocardial Purkinje fibres, causing QRS prolongation, hypotension, ventricular arrhythmias, and seizures. The 12-lead ECG features predicting arrhythmia are QRS at or above 100 ms and a terminal R wave in aVR at or above 3 mm. Treatment is sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg IV boluses repeated to narrow QRS, target arterial pH 7.45 to 7.55, and reverse hypotension. Hyperventilation alkalinisation can be added. Lipid emulsion is rescue therapy for cardiovascular collapse despite bicarbonate.
key_points: QRS at or above 100 ms or terminal R in aVR at or above 3 mm triggers sodium bicarbonate.
Avoid class IA, IC, and III antiarrhythmics in TCA poisoning.
Lipid emulsion is rescue for refractory hypotension despite bicarbonate.
explanation.pearls: Maintain sodium bicarbonate boluses or infusion until QRS narrows below 100 ms and the patient is haemodynamically stable, not until pH returns to normal range.
